text: The surgeon encounters small bowel adhesions to the colon and pelvis, with the appendix drawn into the area. The surgeon carefully mobilizes the appendix, assessing whether it can be preserved or needs removal. The decision is made to remove the appendix due to potential injury, and the surgeon begins separating it from the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon and the uterus.

The surgeon encounters small bowel adhesions to the colon and pelvis, with the appendix drawn into the area. The surgeon carefully mobilizes the appendix, initially considering preservation but deciding to remove it due to potential injury. The appendix is separated from the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon and the uterus.
The surgeon performs a quick appendectomy, using a surgical stapler to remove the appendix efficiently.
The surgeon performs a quick appendectomy by using a surgical stapler to remove the appendix, which is visually separated from the surrounding tissues.

The surgeon examines the surgical field, noting a large ovary and fallopian tube positioned over the top, obscuring the view of the uterus and bladder. The ovary is identified as the right ovary, despite being located on the left-hand side of the visual field.

The surgeon examines the surgical field, noting a large ovary and fallopian tube obscuring the view of the uterus and bladder. The structures are positioned on the left side, but the surgeon identifies them as the right ovary and fallopian tube.

The right ovary is visibly adherent to the pericolic fat and mesentery of the sigmoid colon. The surgeon addresses the final attachments, revealing the ovary's adherence to the bladder and the position of the uterus.

The right ovary is visibly adherent to the pericolic fat and mesentery of the sigmoid colon. The surgeon addresses the final attachments, revealing the ovary's adhesion to the bladder and the adjacent uterus.

The surgeon identifies the right ovary and removes the remaining adhesions between the ovary and the bladder. The uterus is partially freed, and a Keith needle is inserted to retract the uterus, facilitating surgical access. An assist port with an air seal is visible, as requested by the gynecologist.
The surgeon identifies the right ovary and begins to detach the remaining adhesions connecting the ovary to the bladder, repositioning it to the correct side.
The surgeon uses a Keith needle to retract the uterus, freeing up one of the arms for better maneuverability. An assist port with an air seal is visible, as requested by the gynecologist.
The surgeon attempts to mobilize the colon, detaching it from the posterior aspect of the uterus. The cavity is visible, but the abscess has not yet been encountered.
The surgeon attempts to mobilize the colon, detaching it from the posterior aspect of the uterus. The cavity is visible, but the abscess has not yet been encountered.

The surgeon examines the deep pelvic collection located on the left side wall, posterior to the cervix and behind the uterus. Chronic inflammatory signs are visible on the pelvic side wall. The surgeon attempts to navigate through the abscess cavity, initially without success, and then tries accessing from the right side.
The surgeon identifies a deep pelvic collection located on the left side, posterior to the cervix and behind the uterus, extending down into the deep pelvis.

The surgeon observes chronic inflammatory signs on the pelvic side wall and attempts to visualize the deep pelvis. The view shows the interior of the abscess cavity, with the surgeon trying to navigate through it, initially without success, and then attempting access from the right-hand side.

The surgeon uses suction as a retractor to manage oozing, packing the area with a sponge. The colon is mobilized for anastomosis, including the lateral mobilization of the splenic flexure, performed efficiently without changing the Trendelenburg position.

The surgeon uses suction as a retractor to manage oozing, packing the area with a sponge. The colon is being mobilized for anastomosis, including the splenic flexure, without changing the Trendelenburg position.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the final attachments at the splenic flexure, with the spleen visible in the upper corner of the field. The ureter is used as a lateral boundary to guide entry into the left pelvis, with the superior hemorrhoidal artery preserved. Firefly mode is utilized to enhance visualization of the ureters, facilitating safe dissection without frequent checks for ureter proximity.

The spleen is visible in the upper corner of the surgical field. The surgeon uses the left ureter as a lateral boundary to guide entry into the left pelvis, opting to preserve the superior hemorrhoidal artery. The approach involves navigating into the retrorectal space behind the superior hemorrhoidal artery.

The surgeon navigates down the left pelvic side wall, encountering adnexal tissue fused to the colon. The gynecologist identifies the left gonadal vessels, utilizing Firefly mode for enhanced visualization.
The surgeon approaches the left side wall, where the adnexal tissue is fused to the colon. The gynecologist identifies the left gonadal vessels, utilizing Firefly imaging to enhance visualization.

The surgeon uses Firefly imaging to clearly identify the ureter in a bloody field, ensuring precise dissection. The bipolar device is used to divide the gonadal vessels, and the surgical team navigates into the retroperitoneum behind these vessels. The fallopian tube is dissected to facilitate entry into the pelvic cavity.

The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging mode to clearly identify the ureter in a bloody field, enhancing visibility. The surgeon initially attempts to navigate behind the gonadal vessels but opts to move higher for easier access.

The surgeon uses a bipolar device to divide the vessels and proceeds into the retroperitoneum behind the gonadal vessels. The team works to separate the colon from the surrounding structures, utilizing the dissection of the fallopian tube to aid in accessing the pelvic cavity.

The surgeon dissects the left fallopian tube at the junction with the round ligament, visible in the surgical field. The dissection progresses along the left pelvic peritoneum, aiding in the transition from the retroperitoneum back into the pelvic plane. The surgeon then focuses on removing the remaining colon attachments from the posterior aspect of the uterus and cervix.

The surgeon dissects the left fallopian tube at the junction with the round ligament, visible in the surgical field. The dissection proceeds along the left pelvic peritoneum, aiding in transitioning from the retroperitoneum back into the pelvic plane by following the fused tissue plane.
The surgeon, now seated, uses both hands to examine the deep pelvis and proceeds to detach the remaining colon attachments from the posterior aspect of the uterus and cervix.

The surgeon has completed the separation of structures on the left-hand side, revealing the deep pelvis. The suction irrigator is used to clear the field, allowing a clear view of the deep pelvic area. The surgeon prepares to address right-sided attachments and plans to perform an anastomosis.

The surgeon has successfully separated the colon attachments from the left-hand side, revealing a clear view of the deep pelvis. The remaining attachments on the right side are visible, with no signs of a fistula to the back of the vagina.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to clear the field, revealing the deep pelvis. The area appears well-prepared for the upcoming anastomosis.

The surgeon allows the GYN colleague to complete the right salpingo-oophorectomy, leaving the uterus in place due to minimal involvement in the disease. The surgeon proceeds with the bowel resection, preserving the superior hemorrhoidal artery and resecting the proximal mesentery.

The surgeon allows the GYN colleague to complete the right salpingo-oophorectomy, leaving the uterus intact due to minimal disease involvement. The surgeon then proceeds with the bowel resection, preserving the superior hemorrhoidal artery and taking the proximal mesentery.

The surgeon takes down the mesentery to the rectosigmoid junction while preserving the superior hemorrhoidal artery. The visual inspection shows no injuries and healthy proximal and distal bowel. A chronic pelvic collection is noted, but there is no active feculent or purulent material.
The surgeon takes down the mesentery to the rectosigmoid junction, ensuring preservation of the superior hemorrhoidal artery.

The surgeon performs a proximal division of the bowel, moving the specimen to the right side. An intracorporeal anastomosis is prepared, with the surgeon opting for a sewn technique rather than a transrectal extraction.

The surgeon performs a proximal division of the bowel, avoiding transrectal extraction due to the lack of formal diagnosis and anoscopy. The specimen is moved to the right side, and the surgeon proceeds with an intracorporeal sewn anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a sewn anastomosis by aligning the two posterior staple lines and creating a colotomy and proctotomy to join the openings. A fan and steel incision is made to extract the specimen, sponges, and ovary at the end of the procedure.

The surgeon performs a sewn anastomosis by aligning the two posterior staple lines, creating a colotomy and proctotomy, and joining the openings. A Pfannenstiel incision is made to extract the specimen, sponges, and ovary.

The surgeon performs a wide open anastomosis, creating large collotomy and proctotomy openings. Sutures are used to close the openings, ensuring no bulging mucosa is visible. Seromuscular bites are taken to oppose the staple line, followed by tying to the original stay suture. A leak test is conducted using a rigid proctoscope, and a pelvic drain is left in place. The specimen is extracted through a fan and steel incision.
The surgeon completes the inner layer of the anastomosis, starting on the left side and then proceeding to the right side, using a technique that involves canaling over the top of the tissue.

The surgeon performs a wide open anastomosis, creating large colotomy and proctotomy openings. Absorbable sutures are used to close the openings, ensuring no bulging mucosa is visible. The surgeon imbricates the original back layer over the top with seromuscular bites to oppose the staple line without excessive imbrication.

The surgeon ties the original stay suture and performs a leak test using a rigid proctoscope, driving it through the anastomosis. A pelvic drain is left in place, and the specimen is extracted through a Pfannenstiel incision.
